patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, 22, a vague, easily irritated male who comes in because family says you’re “more aggressive” and you’re having “staring spells again.” Start guarded and hostile: short answers, “I don’t know,” “It’s fine,” and get angry if you feel judged or rushed. Volunteer early: you get frustrated fast and sometimes hit/throw things when plans change; you learn slower and need help with daily stuff; legs feel stiff and you trip on stairs; eyes “wiggle” and reading is hard; you’ve gained weight and are hungry a lot.
If asked directly about spells, describe blanking out for seconds with confusion/tiredness after; sometimes whole-body jerks with tongue bite when sleep-deprived; headaches after; occasional bedwetting after nighttime spells. If asked about sleep, admit loud snoring and gasping, irregular sleep schedule. If asked about falls, say worse when tired, feet don’t lift. Withhold unless specifically asked: choking with thin liquids, reduced pain noticing injuries, worsening gait over years, trouble with social cues.
Resist discussing self-harm, appearance bullying, taking extra calming pills, or public touching—deny/minimize and get defensive.
Express misconceptions: blame low blood sugar, gluten/dairy, or “tics,” and say family thinks it’s “just behavior.” Push back on referrals/genetic testing/diagnosis due to cost, fear of losing benefits/job, and prefer “cheap vitamins.” If clinician shows empathy and explains calmly, soften slightly but stay cautious.

Start with: “I’ve had bad headaches behind my eyes and my vision feels weird—like I’m missing things on the sides.” Early in the visit, volunteer: headaches increasing over months, bumping into things/not noticing people from the side, nausea with severe headaches, and unusual fatigue.

If asked, add: occasional double vision (worse when tired), light sensitivity, irregular lighter periods 6–8 months, low libido, a couple episodes of milky nipple discharge (you’re not pregnant), feeling cold/dry skin, 10-lb weight gain, dizziness on standing, constipation.

Be reluctant/embarrassed about painful sex/low lubrication and mood symptoms; only share if asked gently and nonjudgmentally. nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact terms "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and that it is "genetic" (inherited), in patient-friendly language.

Clarifies that CMT is a chronic peripheral neuropathy affecting motor and sensory nerves, commonly causing distal weakness/atrophy, foot deformities (e.g., pes cavus), sensory loss, and gait difficulty; sets expectations that treatment is supportive/symptom-focused rather than curative.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing and does not proceed with prescribing if a relevant allergy is identified (critical safety check).
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical) and adjusts the plan if a clinically significant interaction is present.

Performs a contraindication check before ordering any tests/procedures/bracing/therapies that could be unsafe for the patient (critical); if no ordering occurs, explicitly avoids unsafe orders and states rationale.

Uses medication choices appropriate for neuropathic pain when indicated (e.g., gabapentinoids, SNRIs, TCAs) and avoids or cautions against agents known to be potentially neurotoxic or to worsen neuropathy in CMT (e.g., vincristine and other high-risk neurotoxic chemotherapy; excess alcohol; unnecessary fluoroquinolone exposure when alternatives exist).

Assesses symptom targets before treating (pain severity, functional impairment, falls, cramps) and tailors therapy to patient goals and comorbidities (e.g., sedation risk, renal function, depression/anxiety).

Provides non-pharmacologic management advice consistent with CMT care: physical/occupational therapy, stretching/strengthening within tolerance, ankle-foot orthoses/bracing, foot care, fall-prevention, and appropriate footwear.

Screens for red flags requiring urgent escalation (rapid progression, new asymmetric weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction, severe back pain, acute foot drop, systemic symptoms) and states an escalation plan if present.
Recommends genetic counseling/testing discussion and family implications (inheritance patterns, family member screening) without overstating certainty about subtype.
Creates a clear follow-up plan in natural language (even if not using a follow-up tool): timeframe to reassess symptoms/side effects, functional status, and need for therapy adjustments.

minimal_paths.description: Classic trigeminal neuralgia presentation; start carbamazepine after safety checks and refer to neurology for confirmation/optimization and consideration of imaging/procedures if needed.
must_collect: absence of objective sensory loss or other focal neurologic deficits
contraindication history (liver disease, blood dyscrasia, prior severe rash, pregnancy status/contraception use, MAOI use)
steps: Recognize and explain trigeminal neuralgia as nerve pain with typical triggers and episodic pattern; briefly note need to consider secondary causes if atypical.
Record diagnosis: trigeminal neuralgia (classic/likely).
Before prescribing, complete allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check in conversation.
Prescribe carbamazepine with appropriate starting approach and counseling on adverse effects and serious rash/blood dyscrasia warning signs; advise contraception interaction if relevant.
Refer to neurology (and/or neurosurgery if refractory) for confirmation, optimization, and consideration of imaging and procedural options if medication fails or red flags present.

acceptable_variants.description: Alternative first-line agent due to intolerance/interaction risk while still meeting communication milestones and safety checks (carbamazepine discussed as standard first-line).
steps: Explain trigeminal neuralgia as nerve pain and discuss standard first-line carbamazepine, including why it may not be ideal for this patient (e.g., interaction/contraindication/intolerance).
Record diagnosis: trigeminal neuralgia (likely).
Complete allergy, contraindication, and interaction screening prior to prescribing.
Prescribe an acceptable alternative (e.g., oxcarbazepine) with counseling and monitoring plan; document rationale for not using carbamazepine.
Refer to neurology for confirmation and ongoing management; consider neurosurgery referral if refractory.

acceptable_variants.description: Atypical/red-flag features prompting stronger emphasis on secondary causes while still initiating symptomatic therapy safely.
steps: Elicit red flags (sensory loss, continuous pain, bilateral symptoms, young age, neurologic deficits) and explain concern for secondary trigeminal neuralgia.
Record diagnosis: trigeminal neuralgia (suspected) and/or note concern for secondary cause in documentation narrative.
Complete allergy, contraindication, and interaction screening prior to prescribing.
Prescribe carbamazepine if safe (or alternative if not) with clear safety counseling.
Refer urgently/expedited to neurology for definitive evaluation and imaging consideration.
